transcript: Hi everyone, thanks for having me. So my name is Felix, I'm the co-founder and CTO of Asymmetric Research. We are a Web3 security company and we spend a lot of time securing Solana infrastructure like validators or bridges, as well as like on-chain programs. And in this talk I want to give like uh, want to highlight a couple of key takeaways about the state of Solana program security in 2025. So I think overall we are seeing the space maturing, so like the security of on-chain programs is improving, but we are also seeing some movement in the other direction. So I have a couple of like high-level things I want to call out and then wrap it up with kind of a forward-looking statement, like how how do we make sure we we get this space into an even better shape. So the first thing I want to talk about is a bit like the the onboarding story for new developers, right? So over the last two years, especially 2024, we have seen a lot of like new teams onboarding to Solana that develop new and complex on-chain on-chain projects. And on the one side Solana is lucky that we have like the experienced developer teams who deeply understand the runtime, they know like the bug classes, they know the pitfalls, and they're able to write secure and highly complex code. But for like these new developer teams that maybe come from the EVM world, we see that they still struggle with insecure-by-default APIs and pitfalls. And I know there are quite a few auditors in the in the audience, right, and I think everyone has like these different type of bug classes that they're always looking for if they review code written by new teams, right? It's just stuff that's very hard to get right. We see basically the same mistakes repeated over the last two, three, even four years. My typical example is everything related to instruction introspection, right? Every every time you look at like the instructions sysvar and try to reason about what happened in the transaction before or after you, that's really hard to get right, especially once you involve CPI. Related to that is like the extreme power and complexity of these secp and ed25519 native programs for signature verification, right? They're very easy to misuse, very easy to introduce critical bugs, and I think every developer who has worked with them know how hard it is to make them secure on first try. On the framework side framework side, I mean the one thing that that Robert and Jacob are fortunately addressing is like the pitfalls around the duplicated mutable accounts, right? Due to like runtime behavior and framework internals, you basically just lose modifications as soon as you work with duplicated mutable accounts. And then if you step away from frameworks, you run into different issues like bump seed canonicalization is something that I think a lot of developers struggle with, um, when they newly onboard on on Solana. And I think an interesting example that we can publicly talk about was a vulnerability we recently uh reported to the Relay Link team. So Relay Link is like a intent-based uh cross-chain bridge. And on the Solana side, the intent here was run this ed25519 instruction to verify a signature, right, use that to like protect withdrawal of funds. Um, but the team like didn't like fully parse the instruction format out of the instruction stream. Instead they would just take the signer pubkey as well as the message hash out of hardcoded offsets. So these are like the yellow numbers you see when they access the instruction data. That's completely or that's very broken, so you can relatively easy bypass that, get a full signature verification bypass and steal all the funds in the protocol. Um, I won't go through the full like vulnerability and exploit, but uh you can check it out on our blog where we where we did a write-up uh when the bug was patched. Um, another topic that becomes more and more interesting as Solana becomes more mature and you actually have like on-chain programs that are running on-chain for a long time, right, um, is regression safety. So what we see normally is you have a core developer team that designs and develops the initial version of a product or like a feature. Um, ideally they go to audits, so they are confident in the security. The core developers know the code really well, they have a lot of like insights about all the security critical invariants in the codebase. The auditors have as well, right, because they reviewed the whole thing. You launch into mainnet, ideally you survive, you don't get hacked, maybe the project is actually super successful, and you keep running in mainnet. And then your developers might change jobs, they might retire, or they get promoted to management. And at some point you want to like introduce like this small bug fix, you want to fix like a a weird issue that that's not critical but kind of uh painful, or you want to add like a small feature, right? And that that becomes where it's like really critical. At this point like the the knowledge you have about the codebase might be stale, like the developers that really understand this thing might not be available. So maybe a new developer, like a really smart person, is like adding code to this, just committing a small commit. You might also have like an audit on like this specific changeset, but at this point probably the auditor is also not familiar with the whole codebase, right, because this thing is like two years old. And then suddenly like that small change can actually break like security invariants in other parts of the codebase. Um, and to make this a bit more like um realistic, like one example I wanted to call out was a recent uh vulnerability we we reported in MarginFi, right? So this is a very mature project, has been heavily audited over multiple years, has a lot of like TVL. And this commit basically added a new transfer_to_new_account instruction, which if you review it in isolation is like pretty simple, doesn't have like complex logic in it, does all the signature checks signer checks correctly, seems pretty harmless. It turns out that it doesn't integrate well with MarginFi's flash loan functionality. So what you could do is you take a flash loan, you invoke this transfer_new_account uh handler on the account that has taken the flash loan, and then you never pay it back, right? You never have to pay it back. Instead you just walk away with the money. So this was like a vulnerability that was introduced with a very simple change and could potentially like lead to a loss of like a hundred sixteen million dollar. Um, so I think this shows like that a lot of the stuff we have on-chain is really brittle and it's really important to have like defense-in-depth measures to make sure you don't introduce these bugs like two years after your launch. Um, the other thing I wanted to talk about is the rise of CU golfing, right? And um, as you probably all know, there's more and more interest in optimizing compute unit usage of Solana programs. So think of like the stuff you see on on Twitter or X, like Pinocchio, p-token, HumidFi, also like doppler for like really efficient oracle updates. Um, and on the one side this is great, right? This shows we are getting more and more experienced developers. It's a sign of a maturing ecosystem that like people get comfortable enough with the runtime to actually do this stuff and invest resources in writing optimized and fast code. Uh, however, as like a security team, this also makes you really nervous, right? Like very often heavy optimizations and uh security are kind of at odds with each other. A lot of the CU optimization stuff is basically removing safety belts that you normally have just from like the memory safe language um that you're writing your co- contracts in. So with unsafe code and assembly, you're removing like these memory safety uh uh safety belts. Assembly makes the whole thing harder to review. Um, ownership checks is is something like if you really understand like the semantics of the SVM runtime well, you might think about removing some of these ownership checks because they are unnecessary. Um, and obviously a lot of the stuff you care about is like in the SDK, which is like heavily audited functionality, so we are seeing a lot of like rewrites and reimplementations of stuff that's not fast or not optimized, but where we are pretty confident that it's so battle-hardened that it doesn't have like critical vulnerabilities. Um, and the thing with these safety belts is that you don't need them if you don't write bugs, right? So like the argument could be well I just write perfect code and then like all everything is great. But even if you look at like kind of the projects that are developed by like the best people in the space, so um, you see even the best developers don't always get it right. So the two examples here are like pull requests from Pinocchio and p-token, which are probably the highest quality code uh you can find in the space, right, especially around like CU optimization. And the two pull requests here is like one is a bug where it's like there is an um integer underflow in an unsafe code block which leads to an out-of-bound write in in a logging functionality. Um, the other one is in p-token and uh was basically around a missing ownership check that could lead to loss of funds um during the processing of batch instructions. Um, so if even like these really experienced developers writing these like um really interesting programs struggle with like the security aspects of it, I think the average DeFi project should be really careful before to before deciding to remove like these safety um belts and uh drive fast, right? So keep that in mind um when you want to fully jump on the um CU golfing uh train. Um, the final thing I want to call out is around oracle risks, right? Um, so I think um as you all know like DeFi runs on top of oracles for pricing informations. And um we do think that the risk of off-chain oracle compromises is not really well understood by by a lot of projects in the space. So um by the off-chain nature of oracles you can oftentimes not really inspect where the price updates are coming from, how much you can trust them, and how easy or hard it is to compromise them for an attacker, right? Um, so our suggestion here is that developer should spend much more time thinking about how a corrupted price feed can impact a protocol. And this is I think mostly an exercise in defense-in-depth, right, and again like the safety belt mechanisms where you can introduce features like price limits or like an emergency halt mechanism as kind of like these defense-in-depth mechanism to make sure that like a a malicious oracle isn't able to or like a malicious oracle price update isn't able to completely rug your protocol. Um, a recent example that has seen like surprisingly little of public attention was a compromise of uh the Moonwell DeFi project, which is like an EVM-based uh DeFi project. Um, they lost uh over a million dollar because of a malfunction of an unsupported Chainlink oracle price feed which quoted highly inflated uh prices for a certain asset, which was then abused by an attacker. And while in this specific case the like monetary damage might not be super high, right, just a million dollar, we are pretty confident that like we are gonna see more and more of these kind of attacks or compromises in the future, especially when the on-chain attack surface gets more hardened, right? Because an attacker is always gonna look for like the weakest spot. So um if like your on-chain code is perfect then maybe like your oracle provider is actually the weakest spot in your security story. So uh what do we do about that? Like how how can we help to get the space into a more secure place, right? And um calling out like Robert's and Jacob's talk about the new Anchor development, I think a great way to help improve the space is by building secure-by-default libraries and primitives. So Anchor trying to address some of the pitfalls in the framework around mutable accounts, but also introducing like helper functions to basically do secure signature verification in a very easy to or like very hard to misuse API will make it easier for people to onboard securely onto onto Solana. Um, we also gonna inv- or we are also investing heavily into improved tooling. I mean the previous talk talked a lot about AI agents, but also like tooling like static analysis and the newly introduced Anchor fuzzing is hopefully gonna help to make your pro- uh projects uh more secure against like regression and new security vulnerabilities. Um, and finally we are also working on uh stress testing for oracle compromises and misconfigurations. So ideally developers should be able to kind of stress test an oracle compromise against their mainnet deployment and see what actual damage this could trigger in their programs. Um, so um thank you so much for listening. Um, if you want to read more about the issues I described, please take a look at our blog where we always do like technical write-ups of vulnerabilities we discover. If you want to hear more about security in general, security on Solana, or fuzzing, please come to our Dev Cave. We have a Solana security CTF where you can like play around with bugs like these. And tomorrow morning we also gonna give talks about fuzzing on Anchor as well as um binary decompilation of like Solana on-chain contracts. So if this sounds interesting to you, make sure you step by the Dev Cave uh tomorrow. And yeah, that's everything. So thank you so much.

tags.impact_reason: A security firm highlighting common pitfalls in Solana smart contract development is relevant for developers and auditors in the ecosystem.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a monolithic obsidian obelisk as the Semantic Anchor because it represents the 'battle-tested' protocol—seemingly solid, mature, and complex. The single, glowing, fractal crack symbolizes the 'critical regression bug' introduced by a 'single maintenance commit,' a tiny change that threatens the entire structure. For the homepage text, I integrated '$116M' as a holographic diagnostic projection onto the obelisk's surface. This diegetically quantifies the immense financial risk of the vulnerability, treating it as a critical system alert within the digital world.
visual_idea.description: A colossal, geometric obelisk made of polished black obsidian stands in a dark, cavernous server room. A single, razor-thin crack filled with glowing red, glitching light runs down its surface, representing the vulnerability. A faint, clinical holographic projection displays the text '$116M' directly onto the obsidian near the crack's origin, as if it's a system alert. The background is filled with the deep, out-of-focus racks of a data center, with volumetric shafts of light cutting through the atmospheric haze.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A colossal, geometric obelisk made of polished black obsidian stands in a dark, cavernous server room, dominating the scene with monumental scale. A single, razor-thin crack filled with glowing red, glitching light runs dramatically down its surface, representing a profound vulnerability. A faint, clinical holographic projection displays the text '$116M' directly onto the obsidian near the crack's origin, appearing as a critical system alert. The background is filled with the deep, out-of-focus, intricate racks of a data center, with dramatic, volumetric shafts of light cutting through the atmospheric haze, creating a moody and epic ambiance. The composition is vertical (3:4 aspect ratio), emphasizing the obelisk's immense height and leaving significant negative space above, enhancing its imposing presence. The scene features highly detailed, realistic materials, with a focus on the fractured obsidian texture and the ethereal glow of the glitching light and holographic projection. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic, dramatic lighting.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a monolithic obsidian obelisk as the Semantic Anchor because it represents the 'battle-tested' protocol—seemingly solid, mature, and complex. The single, glowing, fractal crack symbolizes the 'critical regression bug' introduced by a 'single maintenance commit,' a tiny change that threatens the entire structure. For the homepage text, I integrated '$116M' as a holographic diagnostic projection onto the obelisk's surface. This diegetically quantifies the immense financial risk of the vulnerability, treating it as a critical system alert within the digital world.
visual_idea.description: A macro, extreme close-up on the polished obsidian monolith. The focus is on the texture and detail of the glowing red crack as it spiders across the reflective black surface. The light from within the crack illuminates the internal, crystalline structure of the obsidian, revealing complex, data-like patterns. The reflection on the surface is distorted, showing abstract blurs of unseen server lights. There is no text.

final_prompt.prompt_text: An extreme macro, tight close-up, perfectly symmetrical, centrally focused shot filling the frame with texture, on a polished obsidian monolith. The primary focus is on the intricate texture and exquisite detail of a glowing red crack, which spiders across the reflective, jet-black surface. Volumetric lighting emanates vividly from within the crack, illuminating the internal, crystalline structure of the obsidian, revealing complex, data-like patterns that suggest advanced holographic glitch interference, characteristic of a data monolith. The highly reflective surface of the fractured obsidian monolith shows a distorted reflection, presenting abstract blurs of unseen server lights, infused with a subtle holographic glitch effect. There is no text. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), cinematic lighting, volumetric light, depth of field, sharp focus, intricate details, glitch art, sci-fi aesthetic, extreme macro, 1:1 aspect ratio.

editorial.headline: Asymmetric Research Exposes $116M Vulnerability in MarginFi Protocol
editorial.standfirst: As the Solana ecosystem matures, critical regression bugs in battle-tested protocols are emerging as a primary threat to institutional capital. Asymmetric Research revealed how a single maintenance commit placed over nine figures of TVL at risk, underscoring the dangers of discontinuous auditing.
key_takeaways: Asymmetric Research identified a regression in MarginFi that exposed $116 million to a flash loan exploit via a new transfer instruction.
Cross-chain bridge Relay Link suffered a critical vulnerability allowing attackers to bypass ed25519 signature verification and drain funds.
Elite optimization projects like Pinocchio and p-token are introducing memory safety errors by removing Rust's standard safety belts for performance.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The narrative that security risks are confined to inexperienced teams is dangerously outdated. **Asymmetric Research** CTO Felix Wilhelm disclosed that even the most battle-hardened protocols are succumbing to regression failures, where minor maintenance updates dismantle core security invariants. A prime example involved **MarginFi**, a leading lending protocol with significant TVL. A seemingly innocuous addition of a `transfer_to_new_account` instruction failed to integrate with existing flash loan logic, creating a loophole where borrowers could effectively walk away with borrowed funds without repayment. This single oversight exposed **$116 million** to immediate theft, proving that institutional-grade security requires continuous, commit-level vigilance rather than one-off audits.

Simultaneously, the ecosystem’s drive for efficiency—known as "CU golfing"—is introducing a new vector of fragility. While projects like **Pinocchio** and **p-token** demonstrate the sophistication of Solana developers optimizing for the SVM, the removal of Rust’s memory safety belts in favor of `unsafe` code and assembly is yielding critical errors. Wilhelm highlighted that even elite engineering teams are shipping code with integer underflows and missing ownership checks when chasing performance gains. For institutional investors, this signals a need to scrutinize "highly optimized" protocols where the removal of standard safety rails may outweigh the benefits of lower transaction costs.

Beyond the code itself, reliance on off-chain infrastructure remains a dormant systemic risk. The compromise of **Relay Link** via a signature verification bypass and the **Moonwell** incident—where an unsupported oracle feed led to a loss of funds—illustrate the precarious nature of external dependencies. As **Asymmetric Research** pushes for "secure-by-default" primitives within the **Anchor** framework, the clear imperative for 2025 is the adoption of defense-in-depth strategies. Protocols must implement on-chain circuit breakers and stress-test against corrupted price feeds to survive an environment where attackers are increasingly targeting the seams between smart contracts and their off-chain data sources.
